page: Amazon Web Services r8ib.12xlarge is a memory-optimized eighth-generation server instance powered by dedicated Intel x86_64 processors. Running on the AWS Nitro hypervisor, this instance features 48 virtual CPUs mapped to 24 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled, operating at a clock speed of 3.9 GHz. It is equipped with 384.0 GB of system memory, yielding a resource density of 8.0 GB per core.

The instance does not include local NVMe storage or integrated GPU hardware, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached options. To support high-throughput data transfers and network-attached storage performance, the r8ib.12xlarge provides a baseline network bandwidth of 50 Gbps.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the r8ib.12xlarge offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio typical of memory-optimized hardware classes. This resource density is designed to support memory-intensive workloads without requiring the over-provisioning of compute resources. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures consistent processing performance under sustained loads.

Appropriate workloads for the r8ib.12xlarge include large-scale databases, in-memory caching systems, enterprise applications, and memory-heavy data processing pipelines that require substantial RAM and high network throughput but do not rely on local storage or GPU acceleration.
description: Amazon Web Services r8ib.12xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 48 vCPUs and 24 physical Intel cores running at 3.9 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. Built on the AWS Nitro hypervisor, it provides a dedicated CPU allocation and 384.0 GB of system memory, establishing an 8.0 GB per core ratio. The instance lacks local storage and GPU hardware, relying instead on network-attached storage supported by a baseline network bandwidth of 50 Gbps. This hardware profile delivers qualitative cost efficiency for memory-bound applications by offering a high memory-to-vCPU ratio without forcing the acquisition of unnecessary compute cores. The r8ib.12xlarge is engineered for workloads such as in-memory databases, large-scale caching layers, and enterprise applications requiring high memory capacity and consistent network performance.
